toolUseResult.originalFile:
Rossland Works — Annex C

Launch Roadmap

A phased execution plan from site selection through stable operations, with CBT partnership milestones and go/no-go gates at each stage.

Foundation (Months 1-3)
Pre-Launch (Months 4-6)
Launch (Month 7)
Stabilize (Months 8-18)
CBT Reporting Milestones
Foundation
Months 1-3
Pre-Launch
Months 4-6
Open
Month 7
Stabilize and Grow
Months 8-18
1

Foundation

Months 1-3 — Before any money is spent on fit-out
Gate: CBT funding confirmed, lease signed, entity registered
Site Identification and Lease Negotiation
Shortlist 3-5 properties in central Rossland. Target ground-floor visibility. Negotiate 3-year lease with CBT-contingent clause. Ideal: 1,500-2,000 sq ft at $12-16/sq ft/yr.
Critical Path
Legal Entity Registration
Register BC corporation or cooperative. Community Contribution Company (C3) structure may strengthen CBT application. Engage local counsel — budget $1,500-3,000.
Legal
CBT Initial Meeting
Book introductory meeting with local CBT Basin Advisor. Present concept, confirm applicable funding streams (Impact Investment, Working Capital). Get written letter of interest if possible — this accelerates the formal application.
CBT Priority
CBT Formal Funding Application
Submit to CBT Impact Investment Fund. Attach finalized business plan, financial model (3 scenarios), and any letters of support from City of Rossland, Rossland Chamber, or RDKB. Application review typically takes 60-90 days.
Critical Path
Community Demand Survey
Run a 2-week online survey targeting Rossland/Trail remote workers, freelancers, and small businesses. Goal: 50+ responses, 15+ soft commitments. Use SurveyMonkey or Typeform. Share via Trail Daily Times, local Facebook groups, City notice boards.
Market Validation
Placeholder Website and Email List
Simple landing page (Squarespace or Carrd) with name, concept description, and email signup. Begin building a list now — every email is a potential founding member. Target 100+ subscribers before pre-launch.
Marketing
Month 1
CBT meeting completed, survey live
Month 2
CBT application submitted, top lease option identified
Month 3
GO/NO-GO: CBT funding decision expected. Lease conditional on approval.
2

Pre-Launch

Months 4-6 — Fit-out, founding member campaign, systems setup
Gate: 15 founding members signed, space 80% ready
Space Fit-Out and Construction
Hire local contractor. Priorities: open plan with 12+ desks, 1-2 phone booths, meeting room (6-8 person), kitchenette, fast reliable power. Budget $20-45K. Use local trades to build community goodwill.
Operations
Furniture and Technology Procurement
Source ergonomic chairs ($150-250 each), height-adjust desks for dedicated spaces, meeting table, monitor arms. Tech: Gigabit fiber install, Ubiquiti or similar mesh wifi, Kisi or Brivo access control, 85" display for events. Budget: $12-22K furniture, $5-9K tech.
Capital
Founding Member Campaign
Email list launch: "Charter Members get 40% off for 12 months, locked rate forever." Target 15 sign-ups at $75/mo (Hot Desk rate). Revenue: $13,500 in committed Year 1 cash. Host one in-person info session at City Hall or Rossland public library.
Revenue
Management Software Setup
Implement Nexudus, Cobot, or OfficeRnD for member management, billing, room booking, and access logs. These platforms run $80-200/mo and pay for themselves by eliminating manual admin. Set up Stripe for automated billing.
Systems
Hire Part-Time Community Manager
Post locally and on WorkBC. Ideal candidate: lives in Rossland, knows the community, flexible hours. 20 hrs/week at $22/hr. Responsible for member onboarding, daily operations, and event coordination. Non-negotiable before launch day.
HR
Red Mountain and Trail Business Outreach
Reach out to Red Mountain Resort operations, Teck Coal Trail offices, and RDKB. Offer corporate day pass bundles. A single corporate account (10 day passes/month) generates $220/mo. Aim for 3 accounts before launch.
B2B
Month 4
Lease signed, contractor on site, founding member campaign live
Month 5
10 founding members signed, internet and access control installed
Month 6
GO/NO-GO: 15 founding members, CM hired, soft-open date set
3

Launch Week

Month 7 — Open doors and establish the brand
Gate: 20+ members on Day 30, 3 events in first month
Grand Opening Event
Public open house: Saturday 10am-4pm. Invite City of Rossland mayor, CBT Basin Advisor, local press, all email list subscribers. Free coffee, demo day passes, founding member sign-up table. Aim for 60-100 attendees. Budget $1,000 for catering and signage.
Launch
Free Day Pass Week
Offer 5 free day passes each to the first 20 people who sign up post-launch. This fills the space during the critical first week, creates social proof photos, and converts trial users to members. Track conversion rate closely.
Acquisition
Local PR Push
Submit press release to Trail Daily Times, Rossland Telegraph, and CBC Kootenay. Pitch a feature story around remote work and small-town economic resilience. One well-placed article generates more awareness than months of social media posting.
PR
First Month Event Calendar
Book 3 events immediately: (1) CBT Basin Advisor drop-in session, (2) Remote work skill share (productivity tools), (3) Kootenay entrepreneurship meetup. Events drive foot traffic and position the space as a hub, not just an office.
Community
4

Stabilize and Grow

Months 8-18 — Build toward sustainable operations
Gate: Month 12 operating surplus, CBT progress report submitted
Monthly KPI Review
Track: total members by tier, occupancy rate by time of day, meeting room booking rate, net promoter score (quarterly survey), revenue vs budget. Review at start of each month. Act on any metric 20%+ off target within 2 weeks.
Operations
CBT 6-Month Progress Report
Submit required CBT reporting at Month 13 (6 months post-opening). Include: membership numbers, revenue vs projection, community events hosted, employment supported. Strong reporting maintains CBT relationship and positions for future programs.
CBT Priority
Member Retention Program
At Month 6, survey all members. Identify churn risk. Introduce member-gets-member referral: one free month for each referral that converts. The cheapest acquisition channel you have is your existing members.
Retention
Manager Hours Review (Month 9)
If member count exceeds 25 and meeting room bookings are consistent, increase Community Manager to 25-30 hrs/week. Delay this if cash flow is below realistic scenario. The CM is the single biggest operational cost lever.
HR
Year 2 Expansion Assessment
At Month 15, evaluate: Are we at 80%+ occupancy? Is there a waitlist for dedicated desks or offices? If yes, begin planning for adjacent space or second location. If no, focus on non-member meeting room and event revenue to grow top line without adding fixed costs.
Strategy
CBT Annual Report (Month 19)
Full-year CBT report due at end of Year 1 of operations. This is the basis for CBT continued support and potential loan deferral. Include case studies of 3-5 members whose work was enabled by the space. Stories matter as much as numbers.
CBT Priority

CBT Partnership Framework

Columbia Basin Trust has two primary funding vehicles relevant to this project. Approach them as a long-term partner, not a one-time grant source. CBT advisors can make introductions to other regional partners and amplify your network significantly.

Impact Investment Fund
$50K - $500K
Low-interest repayable loans for projects delivering community, environmental, or economic benefit to Basin residents. Coworking has a direct economic development story. Application requires business plan, financials, and demonstration of community support. Turnaround: 60-90 days.
Working Capital Loan
Up to $40K
Short-term financing for eligible Basin businesses covering operating expenses during growth phases. Can supplement the Impact Investment Fund application or serve as a standalone bridge. Faster approval than the main fund.
Basin Business Advisors
No cost
CBT offers free business advisory services to Basin entrepreneurs. Use this before applying for funding — advisors help you strengthen your application and avoid common pitfalls. They also know which local programs can stack with CBT funding.
Hosted CBT Events
Partnership value
Offer Rossland Works as a venue for quarterly CBT Basin Advisor drop-in sessions. This creates a direct link between CBT and your space in community members' minds, and is a concrete deliverable you can name in your funding application as community benefit.
Risk Register
Risk Level Mitigation
CBT funding delayed or declined
Application takes 90+ days or does not meet criteria		 High Parallel application to Columbia Basin Credit Union for conventional small business loan. Founding member pre-sales provide partial bridge capital. Do not sign a lease until either CBT approval or alternate financing is confirmed.
Demand below conservative scenario
Fewer than 15 members in first 6 months		 High Aggressive non-member meeting room and event rental. Reduce CM hours to 15/week. Partner with Trail businesses for satellite desk packages. Month 9 go/no-go review triggers restructuring plan if occupancy under 50%.
Lease location unsuitable
No available space in central Rossland at viable rate		 Medium Scope includes Rossland Community Complex, above-retail spaces on Columbia Ave, and potentially a sub-lease from an existing business with excess space. If central location fails, consider Trail as primary market with Rossland pop-up presence.
Seasonal demand variation
Summer drop-off as ski season workers leave		 Medium Summer programming: weekly workshops and events to maintain foot traffic. Day pass pricing promotion in off-season. Market to Trail commuters and remote workers year-round — these are not ski-dependent. Virtual memberships provide year-round baseline.
Key person dependency
CM departure destabilizes operations		 Medium Document all processes in Notion or similar from Month 1. Cross-train a trusted member as backup manager. Consider a small equity or profit-share arrangement with the CM after 12 months of strong performance to improve retention.
Competition from home office or Trail
Workers prefer Trail's lower cost options		 Low Rossland's identity as a lifestyle destination is a differentiator — the commute from Trail to Rossland is 15 minutes. Position on community quality and mountain lifestyle, not just desk availability. No direct competitor exists in Rossland currently.
Pre-Opening Checklist
  • BC company or C3 registered with CRA business number
  • CBT funding application submitted with supporting documents
  • Lease signed with 3-year term and CBT-contingent clause
  • General liability and commercial property insurance active
  • Gigabit internet ordered and installation date confirmed
  • Access control system (keycard/app) installed and tested
  • Member management software configured with pricing tiers
  • Stripe account active; automatic billing tested end-to-end
  • 15 founding member contracts signed and first payment collected
  • Community Manager hired, onboarded, and trained on all systems
  • Fire safety, occupancy permit, and health authority sign-offs complete
  • Grand opening event date confirmed and invitations sent
  • First 30 days of events booked and promoted
  • Monthly financial reporting template prepared for CBT
Rossland Works — Launch Roadmap — Annex C
Prepared for CBT Partnership Review — 2026

input.new_string:
Founder Prepares to Operate as Community Manager
The founder runs the space from day one at 80 hrs/month ($22/hr = $1,760/mo), scaling hours as membership grows. This means completing onboarding on management software, access control, and billing systems before soft launch. No external hire needed initially — the founder's direct investment in daily operations is the space's strongest asset in the early months.
Operations

toolUseResult.originalFile:
Prism Works — Annex C

Launch Roadmap

A phased execution plan from site selection through stable operations, with CBT partnership milestones and go/no-go gates at each stage.

Foundation (Months 1-3)
Pre-Launch (Months 4-6)
Launch (Month 7)
Stabilize (Months 8-18)
CBT Reporting Milestones
Foundation
Months 1-3
Pre-Launch
Months 4-6
Open
Month 7
Stabilize and Grow
Months 8-18
1

Foundation

Months 1-3 — Before any money is spent on fit-out
Gate: CBT funding confirmed, lease signed, entity registered
Space Agreement with CBT
CBT already owns the 1,800 sq ft target space. The priority is agreeing on occupancy terms — lease rate, access date, and any conditions — rather than searching for a third-party property. A subsidized rate from CBT strengthens every financial scenario. Confirm timeline and terms in the initial CBT Basin Advisor meeting.
Critical Path
Legal Entity Registration
Register BC corporation or cooperative. Community Contribution Company (C3) structure may strengthen CBT application. Engage local counsel — budget $1,500-3,000.
Legal
CBT Initial Meeting
Book introductory meeting with local CBT Basin Advisor. Present concept, confirm applicable funding streams (Impact Investment, Working Capital). Get written letter of interest if possible — this accelerates the formal application.
CBT Priority
CBT Formal Funding Application
Submit to CBT Impact Investment Fund. Attach finalized business plan, financial model (3 scenarios), and any letters of support from City of Rossland, Rossland Chamber, or RDKB. Application review typically takes 60-90 days.
Critical Path
Community Demand Survey
Run a 2-week online survey targeting Rossland/Trail remote workers, freelancers, and small businesses. Goal: 50+ responses, 15+ soft commitments. Use SurveyMonkey or Typeform. Share via Trail Daily Times, local Facebook groups, City notice boards.
Market Validation
Placeholder Website and Email List
Simple landing page (Squarespace or Carrd) with name, concept description, and email signup. Begin building a list now — every email is a potential founding member. Target 100+ subscribers before pre-launch.
Marketing
Month 1
CBT meeting completed, survey live
Month 2
CBT application submitted, top lease option identified
Month 3
GO/NO-GO: CBT funding decision expected. Lease conditional on approval.
2

Pre-Launch

Months 4-6 — Fit-out, founding member campaign, systems setup
Gate: 15 founding members signed, space 80% ready
Space Fit-Out and Construction
Hire local contractor. Priorities: open plan with 12+ desks, 1-2 phone booths, meeting room (6-8 person), kitchenette, fast reliable power. Budget $20-45K. Use local trades to build community goodwill.
Operations
Furniture and Technology Procurement
Source ergonomic chairs ($150-250 each), height-adjust desks for dedicated spaces, meeting table, monitor arms. Tech: Gigabit fiber install, Ubiquiti or similar mesh wifi, Kisi or Brivo access control, 85" display for events. Budget: $12-22K furniture, $5-9K tech.
Capital
Founding Member Campaign
Email list launch: "Charter Members get 40% off for 12 months, locked rate forever." Target 15 sign-ups at $75/mo (Hot Desk rate). Revenue: $13,500 in committed Year 1 cash. Host one in-person info session at City Hall or Rossland public library.
Revenue
Management Software Setup
Implement Nexudus, Cobot, or OfficeRnD for member management, billing, room booking, and access logs. These platforms run $80-200/mo and pay for themselves by eliminating manual admin. Set up Stripe for automated billing.
Systems
Hire Part-Time Community Manager
Post locally and on WorkBC. Ideal candidate: lives in Rossland, knows the community, flexible hours. 20 hrs/week at $22/hr. Responsible for member onboarding, daily operations, and event coordination. Non-negotiable before launch day.
HR
Red Mountain and Trail Business Outreach
Reach out to Red Mountain Resort operations, Teck Coal Trail offices, and RDKB. Offer corporate day pass bundles. A single corporate account (10 day passes/month) generates $220/mo. Aim for 3 accounts before launch.
B2B
Month 4
Lease signed, contractor on site, founding member campaign live
Month 5
10 founding members signed, internet and access control installed
Month 6
GO/NO-GO: 15 founding members, CM hired, soft-open date set
3

Launch Week

Month 7 — Open doors and establish the brand
Gate: 20+ members on Day 30, 3 events in first month
Grand Opening Event
Public open house: Saturday 10am-4pm. Invite City of Rossland mayor, CBT Basin Advisor, local press, all email list subscribers. Free coffee, demo day passes, founding member sign-up table. Aim for 60-100 attendees. Budget $1,000 for catering and signage.
Launch
Free Day Pass Week
Offer 5 free day passes each to the first 20 people who sign up post-launch. This fills the space during the critical first week, creates social proof photos, and converts trial users to members. Track conversion rate closely.
Acquisition
Local PR Push
Submit press release to Trail Daily Times, Rossland Telegraph, and CBC Kootenay. Pitch a feature story around remote work and small-town economic resilience. One well-placed article generates more awareness than months of social media posting.
PR
First Month Event Calendar
Book 3 events immediately: (1) CBT Basin Advisor drop-in session, (2) Remote work skill share (productivity tools), (3) Kootenay entrepreneurship meetup. Events drive foot traffic and position the space as a hub, not just an office.
Community
4

Stabilize and Grow

Months 8-18 — Build toward sustainable operations
Gate: Month 12 operating surplus, CBT progress report submitted
Monthly KPI Review
Track: total members by tier, occupancy rate by time of day, meeting room booking rate, net promoter score (quarterly survey), revenue vs budget. Review at start of each month. Act on any metric 20%+ off target within 2 weeks.
Operations
CBT 6-Month Progress Report
Submit required CBT reporting at Month 13 (6 months post-opening). Include: membership numbers, revenue vs projection, community events hosted, employment supported. Strong reporting maintains CBT relationship and positions for future programs.
CBT Priority
Member Retention Program
At Month 6, survey all members. Identify churn risk. Introduce member-gets-member referral: one free month for each referral that converts. The cheapest acquisition channel you have is your existing members.
Retention
Manager Hours Review (Month 9)
If member count exceeds 25 and meeting room bookings are consistent, increase Community Manager to 25-30 hrs/week. Delay this if cash flow is below realistic scenario. The CM is the single biggest operational cost lever.
HR
Year 2 Expansion Assessment
At Month 15, evaluate: Are we at 80%+ occupancy? Is there a waitlist for dedicated desks or offices? If yes, begin planning for adjacent space or second location. If no, focus on non-member meeting room and event revenue to grow top line without adding fixed costs.
Strategy
CBT Annual Report (Month 19)
Full-year CBT report due at end of Year 1 of operations. This is the basis for CBT continued support and potential loan deferral. Include case studies of 3-5 members whose work was enabled by the space. Stories matter as much as numbers.
CBT Priority

CBT Partnership Framework

Columbia Basin Trust has two primary funding vehicles relevant to this project. Approach them as a long-term partner, not a one-time grant source. CBT advisors can make introductions to other regional partners and amplify your network significantly.

Impact Investment Fund
$50K - $500K
Low-interest repayable loans for projects delivering community, environmental, or economic benefit to Basin residents. Coworking has a direct economic development story. Application requires business plan, financials, and demonstration of community support. Turnaround: 60-90 days.
Working Capital Loan
Up to $40K
Short-term financing for eligible Basin businesses covering operating expenses during growth phases. Can supplement the Impact Investment Fund application or serve as a standalone bridge. Faster approval than the main fund.
Basin Business Advisors
No cost
CBT offers free business advisory services to Basin entrepreneurs. Use this before applying for funding — advisors help you strengthen your application and avoid common pitfalls. They also know which local programs can stack with CBT funding.
Hosted CBT Events
Partnership value
Offer Prism Works as a venue for quarterly CBT Basin Advisor drop-in sessions. This creates a direct link between CBT and your space in community members' minds, and is a concrete deliverable you can name in your funding application as community benefit.
Risk Register
Risk Level Mitigation
CBT funding delayed or declined
Application takes 90+ days or does not meet criteria		 High Parallel application to Columbia Basin Credit Union for conventional small business loan. Founding member pre-sales provide partial bridge capital. Do not sign a lease until either CBT approval or alternate financing is confirmed.
Demand below conservative scenario
Fewer than 15 members in first 6 months		 High Aggressive non-member meeting room and event rental. Reduce CM hours to 15/week. Partner with Trail businesses for satellite desk packages. Month 9 go/no-go review triggers restructuring plan if occupancy under 50%.
Lease location unsuitable
No available space in central Rossland at viable rate		 Medium Scope includes Rossland Community Complex, above-retail spaces on Columbia Ave, and potentially a sub-lease from an existing business with excess space. If central location fails, consider Trail as primary market with Rossland pop-up presence.
Seasonal demand variation
Summer drop-off as ski season workers leave		 Medium Summer programming: weekly workshops and events to maintain foot traffic. Day pass pricing promotion in off-season. Market to Trail commuters and remote workers year-round — these are not ski-dependent. Virtual memberships provide year-round baseline.
Key person dependency
CM departure destabilizes operations		 Medium Document all processes in Notion or similar from Month 1. Cross-train a trusted member as backup manager. Consider a small equity or profit-share arrangement with the CM after 12 months of strong performance to improve retention.
Competition from home office or Trail
Workers prefer Trail's lower cost options		 Low Rossland's identity as a lifestyle destination is a differentiator — the commute from Trail to Rossland is 15 minutes. Position on community quality and mountain lifestyle, not just desk availability. No direct competitor exists in Rossland currently.
Pre-Opening Checklist
  • BC company or C3 registered with CRA business number
  • CBT funding application submitted with supporting documents
  • Lease signed with 3-year term and CBT-contingent clause
  • General liability and commercial property insurance active
  • Gigabit internet ordered and installation date confirmed
  • Access control system (keycard/app) installed and tested
  • Member management software configured with pricing tiers
  • Stripe account active; automatic billing tested end-to-end
  • 15 founding member contracts signed and first payment collected
  • Community Manager hired, onboarded, and trained on all systems
  • Fire safety, occupancy permit, and health authority sign-offs complete
  • Grand opening event date confirmed and invitations sent
  • First 30 days of events booked and promoted
  • Monthly financial reporting template prepared for CBT
Prism Works — Launch Roadmap — Annex C
Prepared for CBT Partnership Review — 2026

toolUseResult.originalFile:
Business Plan — Confidential Draft

Rossland Works

A community coworking space for Rossland, BC — in partnership with Columbia Basin Trust

PreparedMarch 2026
Version1.0 — Ideation Phase
LocationRossland, BC — Kootenay Boundary
Executive Summary Market Competition Business Model CBT Partnership Financials Operations Risks Timeline
01 — Executive Summary

Rossland needs a place where work happens

Rossland Works is a mixed-use coworking space in downtown Rossland, BC, purpose-built for the town's growing population of remote workers, local entrepreneurs, and seasonal "digital snowmads" drawn to Red Mountain Resort. Developed in partnership with Columbia Basin Trust, it addresses a confirmed gap in community infrastructure while generating sustainable revenue.

4,140
Population
With 44% holding university degrees — one of the most educated small towns in BC
$96K
Median Household Income
Well above BC rural average; indicates capacity to pay for quality workspace
0
Active Coworking Spaces
"The Office" opened 2016 but no confirmed active operation as of 2026
$250K
Max CBT Loan
Via Impact Investment Fund — for projects creating community benefit

Rossland is at an inflection point. The post-pandemic shift to distributed work has sent a wave of high-income remote workers toward mountain towns. Fernie has two coworking spaces. Revelstoke's Mountain CoLab has 50+ members. Nelson has multiple spaces. Rossland — home to Red Mountain, one of Canada's premier ski destinations — has none.

The pitch to Columbia Basin Trust is straightforward: a coworking space retains remote workers in the region (preventing economic leakage to larger centers), supports local entrepreneurs, and reduces the isolation that drives talent out of small communities. This aligns directly with CBT's mandate to improve quality of life and economic resilience across the Columbia Basin.

Recommendation Proceed to implementation planning. The market gap is real, CBT funding pathways exist, and Kootenay comparables demonstrate viability at Rossland's scale. The primary work ahead is finalizing a legal structure that maximizes CBT eligibility and securing a suitable downtown property.
02 — Market Analysis

Who actually works in Rossland

Rossland's economic profile is unusual for a town of 4,000. High education levels, above-average incomes, and a culture built around outdoor lifestyle create a natural constituency for quality workspace. The question is not whether demand exists — it is whether it is concentrated enough to sustain a space.

Primary Demand Segments

Segment Profile Workspace Need Estimated Size Likelihood
Remote Professionals Tech, finance, consulting workers who moved to Rossland for lifestyle Dedicated desk or private office; escape from home 50-80 people in area High
Local Entrepreneurs Freelancers, small business owners, creatives Professional address, meeting rooms, networking 30-50 people High
Digital Snowmads Seasonal ski workers who also hold remote jobs Reliable Wi-Fi, day passes, short-term access Variable; 20-40 per winter season Medium
Nonprofit / NGO Workers CBT staff, regional district employees, social service orgs Meeting rooms, occasional desk access 15-25 people Medium
Trail/Greater Area Commuters Workers from Trail (15 km) who prefer Rossland's atmosphere Hot desk, meeting rooms 10-20 people Medium

Key Market Drivers

Tailwinds

  • Remote work is now structurally embedded in the Canadian economy — roughly 40% of knowledge workers have permanent hybrid or full-remote arrangements
  • Red Mountain is a destination resort attracting international visitors; a working-friendly environment is an increasingly expected amenity
  • Rossland's population grew from3,908 (2016) to 4,328 (2021) — a 10.7% increase — well above BC rural average growth, indicating in-migration from urban centers
  • Median household income of $96K suggests the population can absorb $150-300/mo membership costs
  • 44% university degree attainment correlates strongly with remote work employment categories

Headwinds

  • Population base of 4,140 is genuinely small — membership growth has a hard ceiling
  • Large homes with dedicated offices reduce urgency to seek external workspace
  • 10.5% unemployment suggests significant population not in knowledge work
  • Strong seasonality: ski season brings extra demand, summers may be lean
  • Trail (15 km away, population ~7,500) competes for the same regional workforce

The Remote Work Migration Pattern

Data from comparable BC mountain towns validates the opportunity. Revelstoke (population ~8,500) supports Mountain CoLab with 50+ members at $65-130/month, generating sufficient revenue to operate as a worker-owned cooperative since 2015. Fernie (population ~5,500) supports two distinct coworking spaces with different positioning. Rossland's higher income levels and education profile suggest the per-capita demand rate should be comparable.

Bottom-Line Market Estimate A realistic steady-state membership of 25-40 active members within 18 months is achievable based on comparable Kootenay towns. This is the minimum viable scale. Mountain CoLab (Revelstoke) operates healthily with ~50 members — Rossland should target that same ceiling given its smaller population.
03 — Competitive Landscape

What already exists, and what the gap looks like

The most important competitive finding: "The Office" at 2105 Columbia Avenue, Rossland, opened in December 2016 but has no confirmed current operation. The owners, Courtenay and Anthony Jewitt, created it primarily to facilitate Anthony's remote work from Texas — a personal-use model that rarely sustains community-scale operations. Verifying its current status before launch is essential.

Space Location Distance Pricing (Hot Desk) Model Status
The Office Rossland, BC Local Unknown For-profit Unclear
Mountain CoLab Revelstoke, BC 3 hrs $65/mo Worker co-op Active
Fernie Common Fernie, BC 2 hrs Daily/weekly/monthly For-profit Active
2nd Edition Fernie, BC 2 hrs Multiple packages For-profit Active
Baker Street Co-working Nelson, BC 1.5 hrs Monthly desk rentals For-profit Active
Rossland Works (proposed) Rossland, BC — $125-175/mo Co-op or social enterprise Proposed

The distance to the nearest confirmed active coworking space (Nelson at ~1.5 hours) effectively makes Rossland's market a captive one. Anyone in the Rossland-Trail-Warfield corridor who wants regular coworking access currently has no practical local option.

Positioning Opportunity

Mountain CoLab's cooperative model in Revelstoke is the clearest template. It works because low prices maximize access, volunteer management minimizes costs, and the community-ownership structure generates member loyalty. For Rossland Works, a hybrid model is worth considering: structured as a non-profit society or cooperative, but with professional part-time management to ensure reliability.

04 — Business Model

Revenue streams and how they stack

A coworking space in a town of 4,000 cannot survive on membership fees alone. The successful Kootenay model supplements membership with event space rental, community programming, partnerships with local businesses, and — where possible — accommodation for visiting workers.

Recommended Legal Structure

A non-profit society registered under BC's Societies Act is the recommended structure. This is for three reasons:

  1. It makes the organization eligible for CBT's Community Development Program grants (which require non-profit or local government applicants)
  2. It aligns with the community-benefit mandate CBT looks for in Impact Investment Fund applications
  3. It mirrors the Mountain CoLab model, which has sustained operations since 2015 on minimal resources

A for-profit structure is viable but reduces CBT funding options significantly. A cooperative structure (like Mountain CoLab) is a third option that creates strong member engagement but requires more governance complexity.

Membership Tiers

Tier Monthly Rate Included Target Member
Community Pass $50/mo 10 day-pass credits/month, community events Occasional users, retirees, students
Hot Desk $125/mo Unlimited hot desk access, locker, 2 hrs meeting room Remote workers, freelancers
Dedicated Desk $250/mo Assigned desk, storage, 4 hrs meeting room, mail Full-time remote workers
Private Office $550-650/mo Private office (2-3 person), all amenities, priority room booking Small teams, consultants
Virtual $35/mo Business address, mail handling, 1 hr meeting room/month Home-based businesses needing professional presence
Day Pass $22/day Full facility access for one day Visitors, occasional users

Additional Revenue Streams

Event Space
$40-80/hr
After-hours room rental for workshops, meetings, community events. Critical for shoulder season revenue.
Meeting Rooms
$25-40/hr
4-6 person boardroom available to members at reduced rates, non-members at full rate.
Workshops
$25-75/seat
Professional development, digital skills, business workshops. CBT Basin Business Advisors connection adds value here.
Amenity Add-ons
$15-25/mo
Secure bike storage, dedicated locker, expanded printing package, priority room booking.
05 — CBT Partnership Strategy

How to work with Columbia Basin Trust

Columbia Basin Trust is not a passive grant-maker. CBT functions as a community development investor with a mandate to improve the quality of life of Columbia Basin residents. A well-structured ask positions Rossland Works as an investment in regional workforce retention, not a subsidy request.

The Case to CBT

CBT's strategic priorities align directly with what a Rossland coworking space delivers:

  • Economic resilience: Remote workers earning Vancouver/Toronto wages but spending in Rossland inject significant economic activity into the local economy. A coworking space makes Rossland a more viable permanent home for this population.
  • Business development: Local entrepreneurs gain access to professional infrastructure and peer networks that reduce isolation — a documented barrier to small business success in rural communities.
  • Community connectivity: Cross-sector interaction between remote tech workers, local business owners, and nonprofits creates collaboration that does not otherwise happen.
  • Talent retention: Young professionals who want mountain town life but need professional infrastructure are exactly the demographic CBT needs to retain in the region.

Relevant CBT Programs

Program Amount Type Eligibility Fit for Rossland Works
Impact Investment Fund $10K–$250K Loan (low/no interest) Basin-located businesses/orgs; community benefit required Strong
Working Capital Loan Up to $40K Low-interest loan Small businesses in Basin Strong
Community Development Program Variable Grant Non-profits and local governments only Requires non-profit structure
Basin Business Advisors Free Advisory service Any Basin business Immediate — use now
REDI Grants (via RDKB) Variable Grant Via Regional District of Kootenay Boundary Investigate with RDKB

Recommended Funding Approach

Three-Track Strategy

Track 1 (Immediate): Contact CBT Basin Business Advisors for a free advisory session. This builds the relationship before any formal ask and often surfaces funding pathways not publicly listed. Call 1-800-505-8998.

Track 2 (Q2 2026): Apply for CBT Impact Investment Fund loan of $50,000-$75,000 for capital costs (furniture, equipment, leasehold improvements). Frame as community economic infrastructure. Non-profit structure strengthens this application considerably.

Track 3 (Ongoing): Partner with CBT to host Basin Business Advisor sessions at the space — this gives CBT a tangible local presence in Rossland and positions the space as a delivery partner, not just a grant recipient.

Local Government Partnership

The City of Rossland should be engaged early. The city receives an annual REDI grant allocation from CBT (distributed via the Regional District of Kootenay Boundary). A letter of support from Rossland City Council significantly strengthens a CBT application and may unlock direct REDI funding. The ask: a letter of support, and ideally, the city's participation as a co-applicant for community programming grants.

Important Caveat The CBT Impact Investment Fund had a deadline of August 2024 in published materials. Application cycles vary. The first step must be a direct conversation with CBT — not an assumption that any specific program is currently open. Programs re-open and new streams are created regularly.
06 — Financial Projections

Three scenarios: conservative, realistic, optimistic

All figures are in Canadian dollars. Projections assume a downtown Rossland location of approximately 1,800 sq ft, one part-time manager (20 hrs/week), and a lease rate consistent with Rossland commercial market conditions.

Conservative
$47K
Year 1 Revenue
15 members avg, 3 day passes/week, minimal events. Operating at loss in Y1 (-$18K), approaching break-even in Y2.
Realistic — Base Case
$72K
Year 1 Revenue
25 members avg, 8 day passes/week, regular events. Small loss in Y1 (-$6K), profitable from Y2 onward.
Optimistic
$95K
Year 1 Revenue
40 members avg, 2 private offices, active event calendar. Profitable in Y1 (+$12K), strong growth thereafter.

Startup Capital Requirements

Item Low Estimate High Estimate Notes
Leasehold improvements $20,000 $45,000 Depends heavily on condition of space
Furniture & workstations $12,000 $22,000 20 hot desk seats + 4 private office setups
Technology infrastructure $5,000 $9,000 Gigabit internet, access control, A/V in meeting rooms
Kitchen & common areas $3,000 $6,000 Appliances, seating, signage
Initial marketing $2,000 $4,000 Website, signage, launch events
Legal & incorporation $1,500 $3,000 Societies Act registration, lease review
Working capital reserve (6 months) $18,000 $28,000 Operating costs before break-even
Total Capital Required $61,500 $117,000 CBT loan covers 50-80% of this range

Monthly Operating Costs (Realistic Case)

Expense Monthly Annual
Lease (est. 1,800 sq ft @ $12/sq ft/yr) $1,800 $21,600
Part-time manager (20 hrs @ $22/hr) $1,760 $21,120
Utilities (heat, hydro) $500 $6,000
Gigabit internet $250 $3,000
Insurance $200 $2,400
Supplies, coffee, maintenance $300 $3,600
Software & subscriptions $100 $1,200
Total Monthly Operating Costs $4,910 $58,920

At the realistic scenario's Year 1 revenue of $72K against costs of $59K, the space generates a small operating surplus of approximately $13K in Year 1 — sufficient to begin building reserves. CBT loan repayment is not reflected above and should be modelled once loan terms are confirmed.

07 — Operations

Space, staffing, and day-to-day reality

Location Requirements

The space must be downtown Rossland — walkable from Columbia Avenue's cafes and retail, with street presence for organic discovery. A minimum of 1,500 sq ft is required; 1,800-2,200 sq ft is ideal to accommodate a small event configuration without sacrificing desk capacity.

  • Ground floor or easy accessible upper floor with natural light
  • Reliable gigabit internet infrastructure in the building (or ability to install it)
  • At minimum one private room suitable as a meeting room or phone booth
  • Adequate HVAC — Rossland winters require dependable heating

Staffing Model

Year 1 should run lean. A single part-time Community Manager (20 hours/week) handles member onboarding, event coordination, social media, and basic facility maintenance. Founding members can contribute volunteer hours in exchange for membership credits — this is how Mountain CoLab in Revelstoke has sustained low prices since 2015.

Year 2 growth toward 35+ members justifies moving the Community Manager to 30-35 hours/week. A full-time hire is unlikely to be required until membership exceeds 60 active members.

Technology Stack

  • Coworking management software (Nexudus, Optix, or similar — $80-150/month) for member access control, billing, and room booking
  • Keypad or fob 24/7 access system
  • Dedicated gigabit symmetrical internet with guest and member network separation
  • A/V setup in meeting room: TV/monitor, video conferencing capability

Programming

Programming is what differentiates a coworking space from a rented desk. Recommended recurring events:

  • Monthly Lunch & Learn: Member or local business presents on their area of expertise. Free for members, $10 for guests. Builds community and generates low-effort revenue.
  • CBT Business Advisor Days: Host CBT Basin Business Advisor on-site quarterly. Free to attend. This delivers tangible CBT value to Rossland and strengthens the CBT partnership.
  • Skill-share Workshops: Paid 2-hour workshops on topics relevant to local entrepreneurs (bookkeeping, social media, grant writing, photography for small business).
  • After-hours Community Rentals: Space available to local organizations, boards, and groups for evening meetings at $50-80/event.
08 — Risk Analysis

What can go wrong and how to manage it

Insufficient Member Demand
Medium
Rossland's 4,140 population may not support 25+ paying members. Mitigation: pre-launch membership survey; soft launch with founding member pricing ($75/mo for first 15 members); set a minimum viable membership target (20 members) before signing a long-term lease.
Seasonality
Medium
Winter (ski season) will be peak demand. Summer may drop 30-40%. Mitigation: annual membership contracts (12-month commitments at a discount), target digital snowmads with winter day pass packages, aggressive summer event programming to maintain visibility.
CBT Funding Unavailable or Delayed
High Impact / Low-Med Probability
Impact Investment Fund application cycles are not guaranteed. Mitigation: do not make CBT funding a prerequisite. Maintain a parallel path using personal investment, small business loans (BDC, credit union), and pre-sold founding memberships to reach $60K minimum capital without CBT.
Existing Competitor (The Office)
Low-Medium
If "The Office" at 2105 Columbia is still active, Rossland Works enters a market with an incumbent. Mitigation: verify status immediately; if operating, consider approaching owners about merger, acquisition, or collaboration rather than competition.
Lease Cost Escalation
Medium
If the space becomes popular, a landlord may not renew on favorable terms. Mitigation: negotiate a 3-5 year lease with renewal options from the outset; long-term, explore whether CBT or City of Rossland would consider owning the space and leasing to the society at below-market rates.
Founder Burnout / Governance
Low
Non-profit structures can stall if founding volunteers disengage. Mitigation: hire paid management from day one (even part-time) rather than relying entirely on volunteer effort. Build a board of 5-7 people with diverse skills (finance, operations, community). Mountain CoLab's volunteer model works but requires active recruitment.

Break-Even Sensitivity

At $4,910/month in operating costs, the space needs approximately $59K in annual revenue to cover expenses. That translates to roughly:

  • 20 hot desk members ($125/mo) + 5 day passes/week + occasional room rental, or
  • 12 dedicated desks ($250/mo) + modest event revenue, or
  • Any combination reaching $4,910/month in blended revenue

This is achievable but not trivial in a town of 4,140. The first 6 months after opening are the highest-risk period.

09 — Implementation Timeline

From idea to open doors

Month 1-2 — Foundation
Validate demand and structure the organization
Survey 30-50 potential members in Rossland. Verify status of "The Office." Register as BC Society. Engage CBT Basin Business Advisors for free advisory session. Approach City of Rossland for letter of support. Identify 3-5 founding members willing to commit to 12-month membership before launch.
Month 2-3 — Funding
Secure capital and confirm lease
Apply to CBT Impact Investment Fund or Working Capital Loan. Explore Columbia Basin Credit Union small business loan as parallel path. Begin property search — target downtown Rossland, 1,500-2,000 sq ft, ground floor. Set minimum capital threshold ($60K) before signing lease. Pre-sell founding memberships at 25% discount to generate early cash flow.
Month 3-5 — Build-out
Fit out the space and hire
Sign lease. Execute leasehold improvements (paint, lighting, flooring, Wi-Fi infrastructure). Purchase furniture in phases to preserve capital. Install access control system. Hire part-time Community Manager. Build website and coworking management software. Run soft launch with founding members to work out operational kinks.
Month 5-6 — Launch
Open to the public and build community
Public launch event (free to attend, gives tours, introduces founders). Press release to Kootenay Business and local media. Free one-week trial for new members. Host first community event within 30 days of opening. Partner with Red Mountain Resort to list Rossland Works as a recommended amenity for "ski and work" visitors.
Month 6-18 — Stabilize
Reach sustainable membership and refine the model
Target 25 active members by month 12. Host CBT Basin Business Advisor quarterly sessions. Evaluate event space revenue — if strong, consider dedicating more square footage. Assess whether private office configuration is generating sufficient revenue to justify the space. Begin conversations with CBT about long-term facility partnership or expansion.
Key Success Indicator Twenty paying members before or within 60 days of opening is the critical early milestone. If pre-launch commitments do not reach 15, delay the lease signing and extend the validation phase. The cost of a delayed opening is far lower than a year of losses from launching too early.
Rossland Works — Coworking Space Business Plan
Prepared March 2026 — Ideation Phase — Not for external distribution
In partnership with Columbia Basin Trust (CBT) — Contact: 1-800-505-8998 — ourtrust.org

Business Plan — Confidential Draft

Rossland Works

A community coworking space for Rossland, BC — in partnership with Columbia Basin Trust

PreparedMarch 2026
Version1.0 — Ideation Phase
LocationRossland, BC — Kootenay Boundary
Executive Summary Market Competition Business Model CBT Partnership Financials Operations Risks Timeline
01 — Executive Summary

Rossland needs a place where work happens

Prism Works is a mixed-use coworking space in downtown Rossland, BC, purpose-built for the town's growing population of remote workers, local entrepreneurs, and seasonal "digital snowmads" drawn to Red Mountain Resort. Developed in partnership with Columbia Basin Trust, it addresses a confirmed gap in community infrastructure while generating sustainable revenue.

4,140
Population
With 44% holding university degrees — one of the most educated small towns in BC
$96K
Median Household Income
Well above BC rural average; indicates capacity to pay for quality workspace
0
Active Coworking Spaces
"The Office" opened 2016 but no confirmed active operation as of 2026
$250K
Max CBT Loan
Via Impact Investment Fund — for projects creating community benefit

Rossland is at an inflection point. The post-pandemic shift to distributed work has sent a wave of high-income remote workers toward mountain towns. Fernie has two coworking spaces. Revelstoke's Mountain CoLab has 50+ members. Nelson has multiple spaces. Rossland — home to Red Mountain, one of Canada's premier ski destinations — has none.

The pitch to Columbia Basin Trust is straightforward: a coworking space retains remote workers in the region (preventing economic leakage to larger centers), supports local entrepreneurs, and reduces the isolation that drives talent out of small communities. This aligns directly with CBT's mandate to improve quality of life and economic resilience across the Columbia Basin.

Recommendation Proceed to implementation planning. The market gap is real, CBT funding pathways exist, and Kootenay comparables demonstrate viability at Rossland's scale. The primary work ahead is finalizing a legal structure that maximizes CBT eligibility and securing a suitable downtown property.
02 — Market Analysis

Who actually works in Rossland

Rossland's economic profile is unusual for a town of 4,000. High education levels, above-average incomes, and a culture built around outdoor lifestyle create a natural constituency for quality workspace. The question is not whether demand exists — it is whether it is concentrated enough to sustain a space.

Primary Demand Segments

Segment Profile Workspace Need Estimated Size Likelihood
Remote Professionals Tech, finance, consulting workers who moved to Rossland for lifestyle Dedicated desk or private office; escape from home 50-80 people in area High
Local Entrepreneurs Freelancers, small business owners, creatives Professional address, meeting rooms, networking 30-50 people High
Digital Snowmads Seasonal ski workers who also hold remote jobs Reliable Wi-Fi, day passes, short-term access Variable; 20-40 per winter season Medium
Nonprofit / NGO Workers CBT staff, regional district employees, social service orgs Meeting rooms, occasional desk access 15-25 people Medium
Trail/Greater Area Commuters Workers from Trail (15 km) who prefer Rossland's atmosphere Hot desk, meeting rooms 10-20 people Medium

Key Market Drivers

Tailwinds

  • Remote work is now structurally embedded in the Canadian economy — roughly 40% of knowledge workers have permanent hybrid or full-remote arrangements
  • Red Mountain is a destination resort attracting international visitors; a working-friendly environment is an increasingly expected amenity
  • Rossland's population grew from3,908 (2016) to 4,328 (2021) — a 10.7% increase — well above BC rural average growth, indicating in-migration from urban centers
  • Median household income of $96K suggests the population can absorb $150-300/mo membership costs
  • 44% university degree attainment correlates strongly with remote work employment categories

Headwinds

  • Population base of 4,140 is genuinely small — membership growth has a hard ceiling
  • Large homes with dedicated offices reduce urgency to seek external workspace
  • 10.5% unemployment suggests significant population not in knowledge work
  • Strong seasonality: ski season brings extra demand, summers may be lean
  • Trail (15 km away, population ~7,500) competes for the same regional workforce

The Remote Work Migration Pattern

Data from comparable BC mountain towns validates the opportunity. Revelstoke (population ~8,500) supports Mountain CoLab with 50+ members at $65-130/month, generating sufficient revenue to operate as a worker-owned cooperative since 2015. Fernie (population ~5,500) supports two distinct coworking spaces with different positioning. Rossland's higher income levels and education profile suggest the per-capita demand rate should be comparable.

Bottom-Line Market Estimate A realistic steady-state membership of 25-40 active members within 18 months is achievable based on comparable Kootenay towns. This is the minimum viable scale. Mountain CoLab (Revelstoke) operates healthily with ~50 members — Rossland should target that same ceiling given its smaller population.
03 — Competitive Landscape

What already exists, and what the gap looks like

The most important competitive finding: "The Office" at 2105 Columbia Avenue, Rossland, opened in December 2016 but has no confirmed current operation. The owners, Courtenay and Anthony Jewitt, created it primarily to facilitate Anthony's remote work from Texas — a personal-use model that rarely sustains community-scale operations. Verifying its current status before launch is essential.

Space Location Distance Pricing (Hot Desk) Model Status
The Office Rossland, BC Local Unknown For-profit Unclear
Mountain CoLab Revelstoke, BC 3 hrs $65/mo Worker co-op Active
Fernie Common Fernie, BC 2 hrs Daily/weekly/monthly For-profit Active
2nd Edition Fernie, BC 2 hrs Multiple packages For-profit Active
Baker Street Co-working Nelson, BC 1.5 hrs Monthly desk rentals For-profit Active
Prism Works (proposed) Rossland, BC — $125-175/mo Co-op or social enterprise Proposed

The distance to the nearest confirmed active coworking space (Nelson at ~1.5 hours) effectively makes Rossland's market a captive one. Anyone in the Rossland-Trail-Warfield corridor who wants regular coworking access currently has no practical local option.

Positioning Opportunity

Mountain CoLab's cooperative model in Revelstoke is the clearest template. It works because low prices maximize access, volunteer management minimizes costs, and the community-ownership structure generates member loyalty. For Prism Works, a hybrid model is worth considering: structured as a non-profit society or cooperative, but with professional part-time management to ensure reliability.

04 — Business Model

Revenue streams and how they stack

A coworking space in a town of 4,000 cannot survive on membership fees alone. The successful Kootenay model supplements membership with event space rental, community programming, partnerships with local businesses, and — where possible — accommodation for visiting workers.

Recommended Legal Structure

A non-profit society registered under BC's Societies Act is the recommended structure. This is for three reasons:

  1. It makes the organization eligible for CBT's Community Development Program grants (which require non-profit or local government applicants)
  2. It aligns with the community-benefit mandate CBT looks for in Impact Investment Fund applications
  3. It mirrors the Mountain CoLab model, which has sustained operations since 2015 on minimal resources

A for-profit structure is viable but reduces CBT funding options significantly. A cooperative structure (like Mountain CoLab) is a third option that creates strong member engagement but requires more governance complexity.

Space, staffing, and day-to-day reality

Location Requirements

The space must be downtown Rossland — walkable from Columbia Avenue's cafes and retail, with street presence for organic discovery. A minimum of 1,500 sq ft is required; 1,800-2,200 sq ft is ideal to accommodate a small event configuration without sacrificing desk capacity.

  • Ground floor or easy accessible upper floor with natural light
  • Reliable gigabit internet infrastructure in the building (or ability to install it)
  • At minimum one private room suitable as a meeting room or phone booth
  • Adequate HVAC — Rossland winters require dependable heating

Staffing Model

Year 1 should run lean. A single part-time Community Manager (20 hours/week) handles member onboarding, event coordination, social media, and basic facility maintenance. Founding members can contribute volunteer hours in exchange for membership credits — this is how Mountain CoLab in Revelstoke has sustained low prices since 2015.

Year 2 growth toward 35+ members justifies moving the Community Manager to 30-35 hours/week. A full-time hire is unlikely to be required until membership exceeds 60 active members.

Technology Stack

  • Coworking management software (Nexudus, Optix, or similar — $80-150/month) for member access control, billing, and room booking
  • Keypad or fob 24/7 access system
  • Dedicated gigabit symmetrical internet with guest and member network separation
  • A/V setup in meeting room: TV/monitor, video conferencing capability

Programming

Programming is what differentiates a coworking space from a rented desk. Recommended recurring events:

  • Monthly Lunch & Learn: Member or local business presents on their area of expertise. Free for members, $10 for guests. Builds community and generates low-effort revenue.
  • CBT Business Advisor Days: Host CBT Basin Business Advisor on-site quarterly. Free to attend. This delivers tangible CBT value to Rossland and strengthens the CBT partnership.
  • Skill-share Workshops: Paid 2-hour workshops on topics relevant to local entrepreneurs (bookkeeping, social media, grant writing, photography for small business).
  • After-hours Community Rentals: Space available to local organizations, boards, and groups for evening meetings at $50-80/event.
08 — Risk Analysis

What can go wrong and how to manage it

Insufficient Member Demand
Medium
Rossland's 4,140 population may not support 25+ paying members. Mitigation: pre-launch membership survey; soft launch with founding member pricing ($75/mo for first 15 members); set a minimum viable membership target (20 members) before signing a long-term lease.
Seasonality
Medium
Winter (ski season) will be peak demand. Summer may drop 30-40%. Mitigation: annual membership contracts (12-month commitments at a discount), target digital snowmads with winter day pass packages, aggressive summer event programming to maintain visibility.
CBT Funding Unavailable or Delayed
High Impact / Low-Med Probability
Impact Investment Fund application cycles are not guaranteed. Mitigation: do not make CBT funding a prerequisite. Maintain a parallel path using personal investment, small business loans (BDC, credit union), and pre-sold founding memberships to reach $60K minimum capital without CBT.
Existing Competitor (The Office)
Low-Medium
If "The Office" at 2105 Columbia is still active, Prism Works enters a market with an incumbent. Mitigation: verify status immediately; if operating, consider approaching owners about merger, acquisition, or collaboration rather than competition.
Lease Cost Escalation
Medium
If the space becomes popular, a landlord may not renew on favorable terms. Mitigation: negotiate a 3-5 year lease with renewal options from the outset; long-term, explore whether CBT or City of Rossland would consider owning the space and leasing to the society at below-market rates.
Founder Burnout / Governance
Low
Non-profit structures can stall if founding volunteers disengage. Mitigation: hire paid management from day one (even part-time) rather than relying entirely on volunteer effort. Build a board of 5-7 people with diverse skills (finance, operations, community). Mountain CoLab's volunteer model works but requires active recruitment.

Break-Even Sensitivity

At $4,910/month in operating costs, the space needs approximately $59K in annual revenue to cover expenses. That translates to roughly:

  • 20 hot desk members ($125/mo) + 5 day passes/week + occasional room rental, or
  • 12 dedicated desks ($250/mo) + modest event revenue, or
  • Any combination reaching $4,910/month in blended revenue

This is achievable but not trivial in a town of 4,140. The first 6 months after opening are the highest-risk period.

09 — Implementation Timeline

From idea to open doors

Month 1-2 — Foundation
Validate demand and structure the organization
Survey 30-50 potential members in Rossland. Verify status of "The Office." Register as BC Society. Engage CBT Basin Business Advisors for free advisory session. Approach City of Rossland for letter of support. Identify 3-5 founding members willing to commit to 12-month membership before launch.
Month 2-3 — Funding
Secure capital and confirm lease
Apply to CBT Impact Investment Fund or Working Capital Loan. Explore Columbia Basin Credit Union small business loan as parallel path. Begin property search — target downtown Rossland, 1,500-2,000 sq ft, ground floor. Set minimum capital threshold ($60K) before signing lease. Pre-sell founding memberships at 25% discount to generate early cash flow.
Month 3-5 — Build-out
Fit out the space and hire
Sign lease. Execute leasehold improvements (paint, lighting, flooring, Wi-Fi infrastructure). Purchase furniture in phases to preserve capital. Install access control system. Hire part-time Community Manager. Build website and coworking management software. Run soft launch with founding members to work out operational kinks.
Month 5-6 — Launch
Open to the public and build community
Public launch event (free to attend, gives tours, introduces founders). Press release to Kootenay Business and local media. Free one-week trial for new members. Host first community event within 30 days of opening. Partner with Red Mountain Resort to list Prism Works as a recommended amenity for "ski and work" visitors.
Month 6-18 — Stabilize
Reach sustainable membership and refine the model
Target 25 active members by month 12. Host CBT Basin Business Advisor quarterly sessions. Evaluate event space revenue — if strong, consider dedicating more square footage. Assess whether private office configuration is generating sufficient revenue to justify the space. Begin conversations with CBT about long-term facility partnership or expansion.
Key Success Indicator Twenty paying members before or within 60 days of opening is the critical early milestone. If pre-launch commitments do not reach 15, delay the lease signing and extend the validation phase. The cost of a delayed opening is far lower than a year of losses from launching too early.
Prism Works — Coworking Space Business Plan
Prepared March 2026 — Ideation Phase — Not for external distribution
In partnership with Columbia Basin Trust (CBT) — Contact: 1-800-505-8998 — ourtrust.org

Business Plan — Confidential Draft

Rossland Works

A community coworking space for Rossland, BC — in partnership with Columbia Basin Trust

PreparedMarch 2026
Version1.0 — Ideation Phase
LocationRossland, BC — Kootenay Boundary
Executive Summary Market Competition Business Model CBT Partnership Financials Operations Risks Timeline
01 — Executive Summary

Rossland needs a place where work happens

Prism Works is a mixed-use coworking space in downtown Rossland, BC, purpose-built for the town's growing population of remote workers, local entrepreneurs, and seasonal "digital snowmads" drawn to Red Mountain Resort. Developed in partnership with Columbia Basin Trust, it addresses a confirmed gap in community infrastructure while generating sustainable revenue.

4,140
Population
With 44% holding university degrees — one of the most educated small towns in BC
$96K
Median Household Income
Well above BC rural average; indicates capacity to pay for quality workspace
0
Active Coworking Spaces
"The Office" opened 2016 but no confirmed active operation as of 2026
$250K
Max CBT Loan
Via Impact Investment Fund — for projects creating community benefit

Rossland is at an inflection point. The post-pandemic shift to distributed work has sent a wave of high-income remote workers toward mountain towns. Fernie has two coworking spaces. Revelstoke's Mountain CoLab has 50+ members. Nelson has multiple spaces. Rossland — home to Red Mountain, one of Canada's premier ski destinations — has none.

The pitch to Columbia Basin Trust is straightforward: a coworking space retains remote workers in the region (preventing economic leakage to larger centers), supports local entrepreneurs, and reduces the isolation that drives talent out of small communities. This aligns directly with CBT's mandate to improve quality of life and economic resilience across the Columbia Basin.

Recommendation Proceed to implementation planning. The market gap is real, CBT funding pathways exist, and Kootenay comparables demonstrate viability at Rossland's scale. The primary work ahead is finalizing a legal structure that maximizes CBT eligibility and securing a suitable downtown property.
02 — Market Analysis

Who actually works in Rossland

Rossland's economic profile is unusual for a town of 4,000. High education levels, above-average incomes, and a culture built around outdoor lifestyle create a natural constituency for quality workspace. The question is not whether demand exists — it is whether it is concentrated enough to sustain a space.

Primary Demand Segments

Segment Profile Workspace Need Estimated Size Likelihood
Remote Professionals Tech, finance, consulting workers who moved to Rossland for lifestyle Dedicated desk or private office; escape from home 50-80 people in area High
Local Entrepreneurs Freelancers, small business owners, creatives Professional address, meeting rooms, networking 30-50 people High
Digital Snowmads Seasonal ski workers who also hold remote jobs Reliable Wi-Fi, day passes, short-term access Variable; 20-40 per winter season Medium
Nonprofit / NGO Workers CBT staff, regional district employees, social service orgs Meeting rooms, occasional desk access 15-25 people Medium
Trail/Greater Area Commuters Workers from Trail (15 km) who prefer Rossland's atmosphere Hot desk, meeting rooms 10-20 people Medium

Key Market Drivers

Tailwinds

  • Remote work is now structurally embedded in the Canadian economy — roughly 40% of knowledge workers have permanent hybrid or full-remote arrangements
  • Red Mountain is a destination resort attracting international visitors; a working-friendly environment is an increasingly expected amenity
  • Rossland's population grew from3,908 (2016) to 4,328 (2021) — a 10.7% increase — well above BC rural average growth, indicating in-migration from urban centers
  • Median household income of $96K suggests the population can absorb $150-300/mo membership costs
  • 44% university degree attainment correlates strongly with remote work employment categories

Headwinds

  • Population base of 4,140 is genuinely small — membership growth has a hard ceiling
  • Large homes with dedicated offices reduce urgency to seek external workspace
  • 10.5% unemployment suggests significant population not in knowledge work
  • Strong seasonality: ski season brings extra demand, summers may be lean
  • Trail (15 km away, population ~7,500) competes for the same regional workforce

The Remote Work Migration Pattern

Data from comparable BC mountain towns validates the opportunity. Revelstoke (population ~8,500) supports Mountain CoLab with 50+ members at $65-130/month, generating sufficient revenue to operate as a worker-owned cooperative since 2015. Fernie (population ~5,500) supports two distinct coworking spaces with different positioning. Rossland's higher income levels and education profile suggest the per-capita demand rate should be comparable.

Bottom-Line Market Estimate A realistic steady-state membership of 25-40 active members within 18 months is achievable based on comparable Kootenay towns. This is the minimum viable scale. Mountain CoLab (Revelstoke) operates healthily with ~50 members — Rossland should target that same ceiling given its smaller population.
03 — Competitive Landscape

What already exists, and what the gap looks like

The most important competitive finding: "The Office" at 2105 Columbia Avenue, Rossland, opened in December 2016 but has no confirmed current operation. The owners, Courtenay and Anthony Jewitt, created it primarily to facilitate Anthony's remote work from Texas — a personal-use model that rarely sustains community-scale operations. Verifying its current status before launch is essential.

Space Location Distance Pricing (Hot Desk) Model Status
The Office Rossland, BC Local Unknown For-profit Unclear
Mountain CoLab Revelstoke, BC 3 hrs $65/mo Worker co-op Active
Fernie Common Fernie, BC 2 hrs Daily/weekly/monthly For-profit Active
2nd Edition Fernie, BC 2 hrs Multiple packages For-profit Active
Baker Street Co-working Nelson, BC 1.5 hrs Monthly desk rentals For-profit Active
Prism Works (proposed) Rossland, BC — $125-175/mo Co-op or social enterprise Proposed

The distance to the nearest confirmed active coworking space (Nelson at ~1.5 hours) effectively makes Rossland's market a captive one. Anyone in the Rossland-Trail-Warfield corridor who wants regular coworking access currently has no practical local option.

Positioning Opportunity

Mountain CoLab's cooperative model in Revelstoke is the clearest template. It works because low prices maximize access, volunteer management minimizes costs, and the community-ownership structure generates member loyalty. For Prism Works, a hybrid model is worth considering: structured as a non-profit society or cooperative, but with professional part-time management to ensure reliability.

04 — Business Model

Revenue streams and how they stack

A coworking space in a town of 4,000 cannot survive on membership fees alone. The successful Kootenay model supplements membership with event space rental, community programming, partnerships with local businesses, and — where possible — accommodation for visiting workers.

Recommended Legal Structure

A non-profit society registered under BC's Societies Act is the recommended structure. This is for three reasons:

  1. It makes the organization eligible for CBT's Community Development Program grants (which require non-profit or local government applicants)
  2. It aligns with the community-benefit mandate CBT looks for in Impact Investment Fund applications
  3. It mirrors the Mountain CoLab model, which has sustained operations since 2015 on minimal resources

A for-profit structure is viable but reduces CBT funding options significantly. A cooperative structure (like Mountain CoLab) is a third option that creates strong member engagement but requires more governance complexity.

Membership Tiers

Tier Monthly Rate Included Target Member
Community Pass $50/mo 10 day-pass credits/month, community events Occasional users, retirees, students
Hot Desk $125/mo Unlimited hot desk access, locker, 2 hrs meeting room Remote workers, freelancers
Dedicated Desk $250/mo Assigned desk, storage, 4 hrs meeting room, mail Full-time remote workers
Private Office $550-650/mo Private office (2-3 person), all amenities, priority room booking Small teams, consultants
Virtual $35/mo Business address, mail handling, 1 hr meeting room/month Home-based businesses needing professional presence
Day Pass $22/day Full facility access for one day Visitors, occasional users

Additional Revenue Streams

Event Space
$40-80/hr
After-hours room rental for workshops, meetings, community events. Critical for shoulder season revenue.
Meeting Rooms
$25-40/hr
4-6 person boardroom available to members at reduced rates, non-members at full rate.
Workshops
$25-75/seat
Professional development, digital skills, business workshops. CBT Basin Business Advisors connection adds value here.
Amenity Add-ons
$15-25/mo
Secure bike storage, dedicated locker, expanded printing package, priority room booking.
05 — CBT Partnership Strategy

How to work with Columbia Basin Trust

Columbia Basin Trust is not a passive grant-maker. CBT functions as a community development investor with a mandate to improve the quality of life of Columbia Basin residents. A well-structured ask positions Prism Works as an investment in regional workforce retention, not a subsidy request.

The Case to CBT

CBT's strategic priorities align directly with what a Rossland coworking space delivers:

  • Economic resilience: Remote workers earning Vancouver/Toronto wages but spending in Rossland inject significant economic activity into the local economy. A coworking space makes Rossland a more viable permanent home for this population.
  • Business development: Local entrepreneurs gain access to professional infrastructure and peer networks that reduce isolation — a documented barrier to small business success in rural communities.
  • Community connectivity: Cross-sector interaction between remote tech workers, local business owners, and nonprofits creates collaboration that does not otherwise happen.
  • Talent retention: Young professionals who want mountain town life but need professional infrastructure are exactly the demographic CBT needs to retain in the region.

Relevant CBT Programs

Program Amount Type Eligibility Fit for Prism Works
Impact Investment Fund $10K–$250K Loan (low/no interest) Basin-located businesses/orgs; community benefit required Strong
Working Capital Loan Up to $40K Low-interest loan Small businesses in Basin Strong
Community Development Program Variable Grant Non-profits and local governments only Requires non-profit structure
Basin Business Advisors Free Advisory service Any Basin business Immediate — use now
REDI Grants (via RDKB) Variable Grant Via Regional District of Kootenay Boundary Investigate with RDKB

Recommended Funding Approach

Three-Track Strategy

Track 1 (Immediate): Contact CBT Basin Business Advisors for a free advisory session. This builds the relationship before any formal ask and often surfaces funding pathways not publicly listed. Call 1-800-505-8998.

Track 2 (Q2 2026): Apply for CBT Impact Investment Fund loan of $50,000-$75,000 for capital costs (furniture, equipment, leasehold improvements). Frame as community economic infrastructure. Non-profit structure strengthens this application considerably.

Track 3 (Ongoing): Partner with CBT to host Basin Business Advisor sessions at the space — this gives CBT a tangible local presence in Rossland and positions the space as a delivery partner, not just a grant recipient.

Local Government Partnership

The City of Rossland should be engaged early. The city receives an annual REDI grant allocation from CBT (distributed via the Regional District of Kootenay Boundary). A letter of support from Rossland City Council significantly strengthens a CBT application and may unlock direct REDI funding. The ask: a letter of support, and ideally, the city's participation as a co-applicant for community programming grants.

Important Caveat The CBT Impact Investment Fund had a deadline of August 2024 in published materials. Application cycles vary. The first step must be a direct conversation with CBT — not an assumption that any specific program is currently open. Programs re-open and new streams are created regularly.
06 — Financial Projections

Three scenarios: conservative, realistic, optimistic

All figures are in Canadian dollars. Projections assume the CBT-owned 1,800 sq ft space in Rossland, the founder serving as community manager (80 hrs/month, scaling with the business), and a lease rate to be negotiated with CBT as property owner. The model uses the market rate of $12/sq ft/yr as a conservative floor.

Conservative
$47K
Year 1 Revenue
15 members avg, 3 day passes/week, minimal events. Operating at loss in Y1 (-$18K), approaching break-even in Y2.
Realistic — Base Case
$72K
Year 1 Revenue
25 members avg, 8 day passes/week, regular events. Small loss in Y1 (-$6K), profitable from Y2 onward.
Optimistic
$95K
Year 1 Revenue
40 members avg, 2 private offices, active event calendar. Profitable in Y1 (+$12K), strong growth thereafter.

Startup Capital Requirements

Item Low Estimate High Estimate Notes
Leasehold improvements $20,000 $45,000 Depends heavily on condition of space
Furniture & workstations $12,000 $22,000 20 hot desk seats + 4 private office setups
Technology infrastructure $5,000 $9,000 Gigabit internet, access control, A/V in meeting rooms
Kitchen & common areas $3,000 $6,000 Appliances, seating, signage
Initial marketing $2,000 $4,000 Website, signage, launch events
Legal & incorporation $1,500 $3,000 Societies Act registration, lease review
Working capital reserve (6 months) $18,000 $28,000 Operating costs before break-even
Total Capital Required $61,500 $117,000 CBT loan covers 50-80% of this range

Monthly Operating Costs (Realistic Case)

Expense Monthly Annual
Lease (est. 1,800 sq ft @ $12/sq ft/yr) $1,800 $21,600
Part-time manager (20 hrs @ $22/hr) $1,760 $21,120
Utilities (heat, hydro) $500 $6,000
Gigabit internet $250 $3,000
Insurance $200 $2,400
Supplies, coffee, maintenance $300 $3,600
Software & subscriptions $100 $1,200
Total Monthly Operating Costs $4,910 $58,920

At the realistic scenario's Year 1 revenue of $72K against costs of $59K, the space generates a small operating surplus of approximately $13K in Year 1 — sufficient to begin building reserves. CBT loan repayment is not reflected above and should be modelled once loan terms are confirmed.

  • Reliable gigabit internet infrastructure in the building (or ability to install it)

  • Adequate HVAC — Rossland winters require dependable heating

  • After-hours Community Rentals: Space available to local organizations, boards, and groups for evening meetings at $50-80/event.
